confidence.disclosure: Abd al-Qadir is the eponymous founder of the Qadiriyya, the most widespread Sufi order in the world. His life is documented by Ibn al-Jawzi (his contemporary in Baghdad) and by the later Shattanawfi's Bahjat al-Asrar. The arc — Persian birth in Jilan, journey to Baghdad as a young man, jurist and preacher, head of his own ribat, prolific spiritual master — is firm. Many specific miracles and dialogues belong to the hagiographic tradition.

sources: Adb al-Qadir's own Al-Ghunya li-Talibi Tariq al-Haqq
Jacqueline Chabbi, 'Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani' in EI2

journey.event: Born in the village of Nayf in the district of Jilan (Gilan), south of the Caspian Sea, in 470 AH. His family claims descent from the Prophet through Hasan ibn Ali on his father's side and through Husayn on his mother's.

At eighteen he leaves Jilan for Baghdad to seek learning. According to the famous story, his mother sews forty gold dinars into the lining of his coat and makes him promise never to lie. When robbers stop the caravan and ask if he has any money, he tells the truth — and his honesty so moves the chief that the bandits convert.
Studies Hanbali jurisprudence under Abu Sa'id al-Mukharrimi and the leading Hadith scholars of Baghdad. He earns a reputation as a serious and ascetic student.

Withdraws from Baghdad to spend years in solitary spiritual struggle in the ruins around the city — the deserted places along the Tigris and the empty quarters at the edge of town. The hagiographies place his most intense ascetic struggles in this period.

Returns to public life in Baghdad and begins to teach openly. His Hanbali legal teaching attracts the orthodox; his spiritual addresses attract the ordinary people. His sermons are recorded by his sons and published as Al-Fath al-Rabbani and Futuh al-Ghayb.

His teacher Mukharrimi's small madrasa-ribat is enlarged for him by his followers. He becomes the head of a major teaching institution in Bab al-Azaj on the east bank of the Tigris and his students come from across the Islamic world.

Composes his great manual of the religious life, Al-Ghunya li-Talibi Tariq al-Haqq (Sufficient Provision for Seekers of the Path of Truth). It is a complete guide to law, theology, and Sufism for the layman, and circulates widely.

By this point his teaching circles draw thousands. The spiritual chain (silsila) that becomes the Qadiriyya order takes shape during these years, but Abd al-Qadir himself does not formally found an order — that organisation is the work of his sons and grandsons.

Dies in Baghdad on 11 Rabi' al-Thani 561 AH (February 1166). He is buried in his ribat in Bab al-Azaj. His shrine in central Baghdad survives today as one of the most visited Sufi sanctuaries in the Islamic world.
